questions.question: Define prototype matching in pattern classification and explain its significance in computer vision.
questions.hint: Focus on the concept of comparing with representative patterns (prototypes)
keyPoints: Prototype matching is a technique where input patterns are compared with pre-defined prototypes representing each class
A prototype is a characteristic pattern or feature vector that represents a particular class
The input is assigned to the class of the most similar prototype based on a similarity measure
It enables object recognition and classification based on geometric features
Significance: Simple, computationally efficient, and effective for well-separated classes

questions.question: Explain the minimum distance classifier principle with an example.
questions.hint: Mention distance calculation and class assignment based on minimum distance
keyPoints: The minimum distance classifier assigns an input pattern to the class whose prototype is closest
Distance is measured using a metric (typically Euclidean or Mahalanobis)
For each class, a prototype (mean or representative feature vector) is computed from training data
The input pattern is classified to the class with minimum distance to its prototype
Example: Classifying character images (A, B, C) based on prototype vectors

questions.question: What is Euclidean distance? How is it used in prototype matching?
questions.hint: Recall the distance formula and its application in classification
keyPoints: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points in Euclidean space
Formula: d(x,y) = sqrt(Σ(xi-yi)²) for n-dimensional feature vectors
In prototype matching, it measures dissimilarity between input feature vector and class prototypes
The class with minimum Euclidean distance to the prototype is selected
Assumes features are equally important and uncorrelated

questions.question: List and explain the steps involved in prototype matching for pattern classification.
questions.hint: Focus on the workflow from training to classification
keyPoints: Step 1: Feature extraction - Extract relevant features from training images
Step 2: Prototype generation - Create representative prototype for each class (mean or manually selected)
Step 3: Feature extraction for test input - Extract same features from input pattern
Step 4: Distance calculation - Compute distance between input and all prototypes
Step 5: Classification - Assign input to class with minimum distance

questions.question: What is a feature vector? How is it used in minimum distance classification?
questions.hint: Think of it as a numerical representation of image features
keyPoints: A feature vector is a n-dimensional vector of numerical features representing an image or pattern
It encapsulates distinctive characteristics like edges, corners, texture, or shape descriptors
Each element of the vector represents a specific feature value
In minimum distance classification, the distance is computed between feature vectors
The prototype for each class is also a feature vector (e.g., mean of training feature vectors)

questions.question: State two advantages and two limitations of the minimum distance classifier.
keyPoints: Advantages: 1) Simple and computationally efficient 2) Easy to implement and understand 3) Works well when classes are well-separated 4) No iterative training required

Limitations: 1) Sensitive to outliers and noise 2) Assumes spherical/elliptical class distributions 3) Does not account for class probability differences 4) Performance degrades with overlapping classes

questions.question: Explain the working of the minimum distance classifier with a neat diagram. Derive the decision boundary equation for a two-class case.
questions.hint: Derive using the equality of distances from boundary
keyPoints: Working: Extract features → Compare with prototypes → Compute distances → Assign to nearest prototype
Diagram shows input feature vector, multiple class prototypes, distance calculations
For two classes with prototypes p1 and p2, the decision boundary is the perpendicular bisector of the line joining p1 and p2
Decision boundary equation: (x - p1)² = (x - p2)²
Simplified: 2(p2 - p1)·x = |p2|² - |p1|²
The boundary is linear (hyperplane in higher dimensions)
All points on one side belong to class 1, other side to class 2

questions.question: Compare Euclidean distance and Mahalanobis distance in the context of pattern classification. When is Mahalanobis distance preferred?
questions.hint: Focus on covariance handling and when correlations matter
keyPoints: Euclidean distance: Straight-line distance, treats all features equally, assumes unit variance
Mahalanobis distance: Accounts for covariance between features, standardizes based on variance
Mahalanobis formula: d(x,y) = sqrt((x-y)ᵀΣ⁻¹(x-y)) where Σ is covariance matrix
Euclidean is simpler but less accurate when features have different scales or correlations
Mahalanobis is preferred when: features have different variances, features are correlated, classes have elliptical distributions

questions.question: Discuss the preprocessing steps required before applying prototype matching for pattern classification.
keyPoints: 1) Image acquisition: Capture the input image in appropriate format
2) Noise removal: Apply filtering (mean, median, Gaussian) to reduce noise
3) Contrast enhancement: Improve image quality using histogram equalization
4) Resizing: Normalize image to fixed dimensions for consistent feature extraction
5) Segmentation: Isolate region of interest from background
6) Feature extraction: Convert image to feature vector (edges, shape descriptors, etc.)
7) Normalization: Scale feature values to uniform range

questions.question: Explain how prototypes are generated from training data in prototype matching. Discuss different prototype selection methods.
questions.hint: Focus on how representative patterns are obtained from training data
keyPoints: Prototype generation: Create representative patterns from training samples
Method 1: Mean prototype - Average of all feature vectors in a class
Method 2: Medoid prototype - Actual training sample closest to mean
Method 3: Multiple prototypes - Cluster training data and use cluster centers
Method 4: Manual selection - Expert selects representative patterns
Mean prototype is most common: p = (1/N)Σxᵢ for all N samples in class
Choice affects classification accuracy and boundary shape

questions.question: Given two 3D feature vectors: Class A prototype p₁ = (3, 4, 2) and Class B prototype p₂ = (7, 1, 5). For a test input x = (5, 3, 4), calculate Euclidean distances to both prototypes and determine the class assignment.
questions.hint: Apply the Euclidean distance formula step by step
keyPoints: Euclidean distance formula: d(x,p) = √[(x₁-p₁)² + (x₂-p₂)² + (x₃-p₃)²]
Both distances are equal (3), so ambiguous classification
If distances were different, assign to class with minimum distance

questions.question: Consider a 2D two-class problem with prototypes p₁ = (0, 0) for Class 1 and p₂ = (4, 0) for Class 2. Derive the equation of the decision boundary and determine which class the point (2, 1) belongs to.
questions.hint: Set distances equal and solve for boundary equation
keyPoints: Decision boundary: Points equidistant from p₁ and p₂
Using formula: (x - p₁)² = (x - p₂)²
x² + y² = x² - 8x + 16 + y²
8x = 16 → x = 2 (vertical line at x=2)
For point (2, 1): x-coordinate equals 2, so it lies on decision boundary
In practice, either class can be assigned or tie-breaking rule needed

questions.question: A training set contains three samples for Class A: a₁=(1,2), a₂=(2,3), a₃=(3,2) and two samples for Class B: b₁=(6,5), b₂=(7,6). Using mean prototypes, classify the test pattern x=(4,3) using minimum Euclidean distance classifier.
questions.hint: First compute mean prototypes, then distances
keyPoints: Calculate mean prototype for Class A: p₁ = [(1+2+3)/3, (2+3+2)/3] = (2, 7/3) = (2, 2.33)
Calculate mean prototype for Class B: p₂ = [(6+7)/2, (5+6)/2] = (6.5, 5.5)
Since 2.11 < 3.54, classify x to Class A

questions.question: Design a simple prototype matching system for handwritten digit recognition (0-9). Explain the components and their functions.
questions.hint: Walk through the complete system design
keyPoints: 1) Image acquisition: Scan handwritten digit images
2) Preprocessing: Binarize, remove noise, normalize size (e.g., 20x20)
3) Feature extraction: Use zoning, Fourier descriptors, or simple pixel values as features
4) Prototype storage: Store 10 prototypes (one per digit), each is mean feature vector from training samples
5) Classification: For test digit, extract features, compute Euclidean distance to all 10 prototypes, assign to closest digit
6) Performance: Report accuracy on test set
Enhancement: Use multiple prototypes per class or k-nearest neighbors

questions.question: What is the difference between nearest neighbor classifier and minimum distance classifier?
questions.hint: Focus on prototype-based vs. sample-based classification
keyPoints: Minimum distance classifier uses class prototypes (representative vectors) for comparison
Nearest neighbor classifier compares with all training samples directly
Minimum distance is faster (compares with k prototypes vs N samples)
Nearest neighbor can handle irregular class shapes, minimum distance assumes convex classes
Nearest neighbor is more flexible but computationally expensive
k-NN is an extension that considers k nearest training samples
